rescue_reasoning: Although the excerpt lacks an explicit mitigation outcome token, the measures described are inherently energy-efficiency/electricity-system measures that function as mitigation. Verbatim text: 'rationalisation of the production of electricity' and 'rationalisation of energy consumption... [including] the introduction of energy certification' indicate improving efficiency and managing demand. Parent context (climate_screen=explicit_parent) explicitly states the protocol 'coordinates policies aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions,' making the sub-statement mitigation-linked. Thus the gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token found') reflects overly strict token matching, not lack of climate relevance.
policy_statement: The Environmental Energy Plan will include rationalisation of electricity production and rationalisation of energy consumption, including potential introduction of energy certification for the civil sector.
mechanism_description: The plan promotes energy certification for the civil sector to provide information that encourages energy efficiency improvements and lowers energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Information disclosure/market signal → building efficiency actions → ↓ final energy demand → ↓ fossil energy combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: Building owners/managers (and the real-estate market) receive standardized energy performance information (labels/certificates), influencing purchase/lease decisions and prompting retrofits, equipment upgrades, and improved operations. (2) Mediating process: Improved transparency corrects information asymmetry and shifts market preferences toward efficient buildings, increasing adoption of efficiency measures and reducing wasted energy. (3) Outcome: Lower electricity and heating/cooling fuel use, yielding reduced CO₂ (and associated) emissions from energy generation and onsite combustion.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption leading to lower upstream fossil-fuel combustion and thus lower GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: information/labeling → behavior and investment shifts → reduced energy use → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While certification can also support cost savings (resource efficiency framing) or comfort/resilience co-benefits, the dominant climate-relevant effect across such programs is emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand.  
3) Edge cases applied: If certification is used mainly for adaptation (e.g., heat-risk labels) or purely administrative tracking without driving efficiency actions, the mitigation effect could be weaker—but the stated mechanism explicitly targets efficiency improvements and lower energy use.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting “energy certification program → market signal for efficiency” relies on performance information to drive efficiency actions; the primary climate impact is therefore mitigation through reduced energy consumption and emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether impacts occur via electricity vs. heating fuels, operational improvements vs. capital retrofits, and the magnitude of realized savings depends on policy design and local grid/fuel mix, and should be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the certification is purely informational with no disclosure at transaction points, weak compliance, or no market uptake, realized mitigation may be limited; conversely, if paired with mandatory upgrades triggered by poor ratings, the mechanism blends into direct efficiency standards (still mitigation).
key_indicators: “Environmental Energy Plan shall be drawn up”; “rationalisation of energy consumption”; “civil sector”; “introduction of energy certification”; “rationalisation of the production of electricity”
co_benefits: Reduced energy bills/cost savings; improved indoor comfort and air quality (via efficiency improvements); potential energy security benefits from lower demand
instance_edge_case_notes: “Rationalisation” of electricity production/energy consumption could encompass regulatory standards or investments, but the text only specifies a potential instrument (“energy certification”), so classification is anchored to information disclosure; “eventually to include” indicates it may be planned rather than already implemented.
